A Family History

Jeremiah Collins

Musician, Company G, 150th New York Infantry — "The Dutchess County Regiment"

1862 — 1865 Poughkeepsie to the Sea Written in 1903 Shared with love by his descendants

Jeremiah's journey spanned roughly 3,000 miles over three years — from his enlistment in Poughkeepsie, NY, down to Maryland, to Gettysburg, west by rail to Tennessee, then south through Georgia on Sherman's March to the Sea, into the Carolinas, and finally north to Washington for the Grand Review before sailing home up the Hudson to Poughkeepsie.

✦ Click any location marker to read Jeremiah's story from that place

Jeremiah's Route · September 1862 – June 1865
Camp / garrison
Major battle
March / campaign stop
Muster out / return
Jeremiah Collins
Musician · Drummer · Company G, 150th N.Y. Infantry

Born of Irish parentage in 1846, Jeremiah enlisted as a 15-year-old boy — the youngest in the regiment — and emerged three years later a battle-hardened veteran who had marched from Poughkeepsie to Savannah and back. He wrote these reminiscences in 1903 — nearly forty years after the war — for the committee preparing the regiment's official history. As the regimental history noted, he "managed somehow to have a more varied experience and see more adventure than some who were older."

Born
20 Oct 1846
Birthplace
Paris, France (claimed)
Enlisted
Sep 15, 1862
Age at enlistment
15 years old · 5 ft tall
Eyes / Hair
Blue eyes · Brown hair
Mustered out
Jun 8, 1865
Died
23 Oct 1909 · Manhattan
Married
Adelaide Ellen Francis · 25 Oct 1868

The Man Behind the Drum — His Own Words

At enlistment Jeremiah listed his occupation as waiter — but before that he had been a newsboy on the railroad, a fact that shaped his restless resourcefulness throughout the war. His service, as the regimental history put it, was "not by any means limited to the pounding of the sheepskin." He acted as orderly for Chaplain Bartlett when visiting Southern towns, was detailed to manage the regiment's mail in Atlanta, and repeatedly rode out as one of Sherman's Bummers — the foragers who kept the army fed on the March to the Sea.

On October 24, 1867, Jeremiah became a naturalized citizen in the Superior Court of New York County. His address was 441 Fourth Avenue — and his neighbor at 438 Fourth Avenue, who witnessed the naturalization, was Richard Croker, the future Tammany boss. The two men would remain close. On February 6, 1867, Jeremiah had already been elected Corporal of H Company, Third Infantry Regiment, 2nd Brigade, New York National Guard.

Three Famous Stories From His Own Mouth

The Hardtack Drum at Gettysburg. During the battle, a supply wagon was hit by a shell, scattering hardtack across the road. Most of the regiment had gone without food for their entire time at Gettysburg. Jeremiah saw his chance — his drumhead had developed a hole from constant beating, making it a perfect basket. He stuffed it full of hardtack before the Provost Guard could stop him. "I was quite popular among my comrades for a time," he recalled.

The Cavalry Revolver at Resaca. At the Battle of Resaca, Jeremiah picked up a fallen cavalry Colt revolver and belt — "just what he had wished for, for he could carry it without its interfering with his drum." Months later, foraging with Sherman's Bummers, he was suddenly confronted by a body of enemy soldiers. That same revolver let him hold them off long enough to escape.

The River Crossing Under Fire. After the escape, he found himself cut off by rebel cavalry. He swam his horse through a river under fire — it was Colonel A. B. Smith's own horse he was riding that day. "Upon their return to the regiment they were received with joy, for they had been given up as lost." The Colonel was "specially glad at their return" — for his horse's sake.

A Career Built on Service

After mustering out, Jeremiah built a life of civic service in New York City:

1865–1883
News agent, NY New Haven & Hartford Railroad
1867
Corporal, NY National Guard, 3rd Infantry
1883
Captain, Street Cleaning Dept., New York City
1886
Advanced to the Fire Department
Final years
Court officer, NY Supreme Court · confidant of Tammany boss Richard Croker

His Family — A Legacy of Public Service

Jeremiah married Adelaide Ellen Francis — born in the Alsace region of Germany — at St. John the Evangelist Church in New York on October 25, 1868. Eleven children were born to them, of whom five survived to adulthood. The family the descendants document invokes a blessing: "may his tribe increase" — and increase it did.

His father Cornelius Francis Collins I had emigrated from Ireland, married Ellen McCarthy (also Irish-born), and died young in Manhattan in 1857 at age 45. Jeremiah grew up in a city of immigrants and made himself, by the end of his life, a fixture of its institutions.

ELDEST SON
Judge Cornelius F. Collins II
Born 1869. Columbia Law School. NY State Assembly. First Asst. Attorney-General. 17 years on the General Sessions bench — presided over the Two-Gun Crowley murder trial and the Vera Stretz case. Drafted New York's first Children's Act. Founder of Catholic Big Brothers. Died 1947.
YOUNGEST SON
Surrogate William T. Collins
Born 1886. Served nearly 30 years on the bench — NY Supreme Court Justice, then Surrogate of New York County. Acting Mayor of New York City (1925). Knight of Malta (Pope Pius XII, 1949). His funeral at St. Patrick's Cathedral was celebrated by Cardinal Spellman; 1,000 attended, headed by Mayor Wagner. Died 1961.
DAUGHTER
Adelaide C. Collins Costigan
Born 1891. New York State Democratic Committeewoman. Active in Queens politics for decades. Died 1968.
CHILDREN LOST YOUNG
Grief alongside legacy
Of Jeremiah's eleven children, six died young — Mary (1870, age 0), Joseph (1875, age 1), Jeremiah Jr. (1883, age 7 — acute rheumatism), Christina (1878, diphtheria), Ellen (1883), John (1885). Infant and child mortality was a grim reality even in a successful New York family.

The 150th New York Infantry — "The Dutchess County Regiment"

Organized entirely from Dutchess County, New York, and led by Colonel John H. Ketcham of Dover, this regiment was regarded as unusually cohesive — many of its men were neighbors, friends, and relatives who had grown up together. It mustered in on October 10, 1862 and was mustered out June 8, 1865 — serving the full duration of the war.

Recruited to
1,277 men
Total casualties
207
Killed in battle
46 men
Disease deaths
81 men
Major campaigns
Gettysburg · Atlanta · March to the Sea
About this document: Jeremiah Collins wrote these pages in 1903 for the "Committee on Revision" overseeing the preparation of a regimental history of the 150th New York — eventually published in 1907 as The Dutchess County Regiment. Jeremiah noted he never kept a diary, so he wrote entirely from memory forty years after the fact. These reminiscences were made available by his great-grandson, Vincent A. Powell. Family history data from the Descendants of Cornelius Francis Collins I genealogical document. The post-war photograph of Jeremiah appears on the final page of his original manuscript.

Descendants of Michael Mitchell Powers

An Irish immigrant family — from County Waterford to the American frontier

Generation 1
①
Michael Mitchell Powers b. 15 May 1846, Waterford, Ireland · d. 12 Dec 1916, Paola, KS

Michael married Annie Maloney (daughter of Patrick Henry Maloney and Mary Ellen Walsh) on 7 Jul 1879 in Paola, Miami, Kansas. Annie was born 7 Jun 1857 in Fayetteville, Brown County, Ohio, and died 27 Sep 1941 at 504 East Shawnee St., Paola, Miami, KS, aged 84.

Arrived 5 June 1849, Port of New York from Liverpool aboard the ship Ward Chapman with parents and brothers and sisters. Per his obituary he came to America in 1849 with his parents and moved west from the "Atlantic coast" with the family in 1860 settling in Appleton, WI. He was a railroad bridge carpenter and moved to Paola in 1876 working for the Frisco railroad. He died of heart disease.
Family Notes
Military Service
Co. H, 39th Regiment Infantry, WI
Enlisted
May 23, 1864 · Oconto, WI
Mustered Out
Sep 22, 1864 · Camp Washburn, WI
Burial
Holy Cross Cemetery, Paola, KS · Lot 171
📰
Obituary — Western Spirit, 15 December 1916

"Michael Mitchell Powers died at his home in Paola, No. 502 East Shawnee street, at 4:20 o'clock A.M., on Tuesday, December 12th, 1916, aged 70 years, 6 months and 27 days… Born in the County Waterford, Ireland, May 15th, 1846, the boy, Michael, was brought by his parents, Mr. and Mrs. Thomas Powers, to America in 1849… and when 17 years old enlisted in Company H, 39th Wisconsin Infantry… He became a bridge carpenter and followed the business until 1905, when he had to retire on account of heart affection. His trade brought him to Miami county in 1876, and here he became acquainted with Miss Annie Maloney."

Mike Powers was described as "a man among men, a moral, brave, industrious American of Irish blood." He was an officer in the local council of Knights of Columbus, a member of McCaslin Post, G.A.R., and a devout member of the Catholic church. Five grandchildren were the crown of his old days.

📰
Obituary — Miami Republican, 15 December 1916

Mr. Powers was survived by his wife Annie and two sons and three daughters: T. E. Powers of Beatrice, Nebraska; Perry Powers of Kankakee, Ill.; Mrs. Wallace Hainline of Paola; Mrs. Charles H. Dunaway of Osawatomie; and Miss Anna Powers of Paola. Funeral services were held from Holy Trinity Catholic Church. The remains were buried in Holy Cross Cemetery.

Generation 2 — Children of Michael Mitchell Powers
②
Mary Ellen Powers b. 28 Mar 1880, Paola, KS · d. 09 May 1960, Paola, KS (age 80)

Married Wallace Millard Hainline (son of Weeden Calvin Hainline and Samantha Jane Paisley) on 24 Jul 1902 in Paola. He was born 13 Aug 1874 in Levee, Montgomery, Kentucky, and died 03 Apr 1946 in Paola.

Children: Herbert Claire Hainline (b. 23 Jul 1903); Wallace Francis Hainline (b. 25 Nov 1906)

③
Thomas Edward Powers b. 19 Mar 1882, Paola, KS · d. 04 Dec 1951, Kansas City, MO

Married (1) Margaret Anna Kerns (daughter of John M. Kerns and Eleanor Harney) on 01 Jun 1915 in Superior, Nebraska. She was born 07 Mar 1879 in Sellersville, Bucks, Pennsylvania, and died 31 Oct 1919 in Beatrice, Nebraska.

Married (2) Helen A. Forster (daughter of George Forster and Louisa Engle) on 20 Jul 1925 in Osawatomie, KS. She was born 01 Jul 1881 in Wausau, Wisconsin, and died 20 Apr 1970 in Kansas City, MO.

Worked for Wherret Mize Drug Co., Atchison, KS. Last address: 5413 Virginia Ave., Kansas City, MO. Per his 1951 obituary he was made an honorary director and president of the University of Kansas City School of Pharmacy Alumni Association.

Child with Margaret Kerns: Eleanor Rosemary Powers (b. 30 Mar 1916, Concordia, KS; d. 09 Aug 1997, Waterford, CT)

④
Grace Agnes Powers b. 20 Oct 1884, Paola, KS · d. 12 Mar 1973, St. Louis, MO

Married Charles Harris Dunaway on 20 Feb 1909 in Paola. He was born 17 Jul 1883 in Chancellorville, Menard, Illinois, and died 11 May 1954 in Kansas City, MO.

Children: Jeanne E. Dunaway (b. 25 Sep 1910, Osawatomie, KS; d. 11 Nov 2005, Tampa, FL); Robert William Dunaway (b. 27 Oct 1915, Osawatomie, KS; d. 27 Dec 1976, Tampa, FL)

⑤
Pierce Mitchell "Perry" Powers Kankakee, Illinois

Known as "Perry Powers." Appeared in Kankakee City Directories 1922–1935 as a "bill poster" for Kankakee Poster Advertising Co. Living at 1457 East Oak Street with wife Frances. Recorded in the 1930 Census in Kankakee, IL.

⑤
Anna Elizabeth Powers b. 12 Jun 1888, Paola, KS · d. 13 Jan 1971, Royal Oak, MI (age 82)

Married Bernard Long Sheridan (son of John C. Sheridan and Martha Caroline Long) on 27 Dec 1919 in Beatrice, Nebraska. He was born 06 May 1887 in Paola, KS, and died 18 Dec 1952 in Paola, KS. In 1941 Bernard was President of the Kansas State Bar Association.

Children: Francis M. Sheridan (b. 11 Feb 1923; d. 26 Feb 1989, Royal Oak, MI); Kathleen Sheridan (b. 21 Mar 1927, Paola, KS; d. 19 Aug 1966, San Mateo, CA); Bernard Powers Sheridan (b. 12 Oct 1929; d. 07 Feb 1992)

Mom and Dad are buried in Gate of Heaven Cemetery, Hawthorne, NY — Section 22, Plot 8, Grave 16.

Source: Descendants of Michael Mitchell Powers — a genealogical record compiled from family documents, Civil War pension records, cemetery records at Holy Cross Cemetery (Paola, KS, Lot 171), newspaper obituaries from the Western Spirit and Miami Republican (15 December 1916), and U.S. Census records. Michael Mitchell Powers arrived in America aboard the Ward Chapman from Liverpool, 5 June 1849.
